original_abstract:
The Trivalent Actinide Lanthanide Separation with Phosphorus-reagent Extraction from Aqueous Komplexes (TALSPEAK) process, developed in the 1960s at Oak Ridge National Laboratory, remains a primary benchmark for the partitioning of trivalent f-elements. By utilizing the selective extraction of lanthanides into an organic phase (typically HDEHP) while retaining actinides in an aqueous phase via aminopolycarboxylate complexants (primarily DTPA), the process achieves significant separation factors. Despite its efficiency, large-scale industrial adoption has been hindered by rigorous pH requirements, high buffer concentrations, and suboptimal phase transfer kinetics.

This review synthesizes a decade of fundamental scientific advancements aimed at resolving these long-standing operational challenges. Recent thermodynamic modeling and experimental investigations reveal that the process’s steep pH dependence is largely driven by the independent partitioning of lactic acid and sodium into the organic phase, which competitively inhibits the extractant. Molecular-scale studies—including FTIR, NMR, and Small Angle Neutron Scattering (SANS)—have clarified the speciation of extracted complexes, identifying a transition from monomeric 1:6 metal-extractant species to polynuclear aggregated clusters under high loading conditions. Furthermore, aqueous kinetic studies suggest that lactate acts as a phase transfer catalyst by facilitating the formation of transient, hydrophobic tris-lactate species at the interface. These fundamental insights have led to the development of "Advanced TALSPEAK" concepts, which utilize alternative extractants like HEH[EHP] and weaker complexants such as HEDTA to achieve more robust pH stability and faster kinetics. This report provides a comprehensive overview of the chemical interactions and supramolecular organization that govern this essential separation chemistry.
humanized_abstract:
One of the most widely used techniques for the separation of f-elements developed in the 1960s in the laboratories of Oak Ridge National Laboratory is the Trivalent Actinide Lanthanide Separation with Phosphorus-reagent Extraction from Aqueous Komplexes process, generally referred to by the acronym TALSPEAK. In this process the lanthanides are selectively extracted into an organic phase (typically HDEHP) relative to the trivalent actinides, which are retained in the aqueous phase as DTPA complexes. While this technique can produce very high separation factors the practical application of the process is severely limited due to high pH values, large amounts of buffer salts necessary to achieve them and slow extraction/re-extraction kinetics.

This review summarizes a decade of work to address the so-called 'Actinide Problem' of separation and recycling of uranides in the nuclear fuel cycle, defined by the requirement for a quantitative removal from the acidic extractant solution in less than an hour over a pH range less than one unit in length. Recent breakthroughs at the thermodynamic and experimental levels were obtained on understanding the reason of the "steep" pH profile observed with most diamine/ malonamide combinations and on identifying conditions in which the full potential of such ligands can be used for the TALSPEAK (TRApentadentate Lanthanide Separation by a Polyaminedonors Extraction Kit) process. By using solution phase techniques including FTIR and solid state NMR and Small Angle Neutron Scattering (SANS), it has been demonstrated at the molecular level that the extracted species consisting of a 1:6 lanthanide/extractant complexes evolve from pure monomeric entities toward poly-nuclear clusters as the lanthanide loading is increased. In the aqueous phase, measurements of the influence of lactate upon the rate of complexation between tris lactate and U(IV) gave evidence that lactate acts as a catalyst because it generates a short-lived tris lactate hydrophobic complex facilitating the diffusion of the complexes towards the organic phase. These fundamental data permitted the implementation of new 'Advanced TALSPEAK' ideas which make use of other extractants (HEH[EHP]) and weaker complexes (HEDTA) to produce pH profiles of great width and with the highest possible extract performances and kinetics.

document.output:
One of the most widely used techniques for the separation of f-elements developed in the 1960s in the laboratories of Oak Ridge National Laboratory is the Trivalent Actinide Lanthanide Separation with Phosphorus-reagent Extraction from Aqueous Komplexes process, generally referred to by the acronym TALSPEAK. document.input:
The Trivalent Actinide Lanthanide Separation with Phosphorus-reagent Extraction from Aqueous Komplexes (TALSPEAK) process, developed in the 1960s at Oak Ridge National Laboratory, remains a primary benchmark for the partitioning of trivalent f-elements.
